title: Budd-Chiari Syndrome
oneLiner: Hepatic vein outflow obstruction causing hepatic congestion, painful hepatomegaly, and ascites; rare but rapidly fatal if missed.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute liver failure:** asterixis + INR >2.0 + hypoglycaemia – this is not simple ascites; needs urgent senior review and transfer to HDU/ICU
- **Variceal bleeding:** haematemesis or melaena with haemodynamic instability – present in chronic Budd-Chiari with portal hypertension; require immediate resuscitation and endoscopic therapy
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP):** abdominal pain + fever + raised WCC in ascitic fluid – sepsis can tip into shock quickly; treat empirically before culture results
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Acute onset right upper quadrant (RUQ) pain (often severe, mimicking cholecystitis)
  - Rapidly increasing abdominal girth (ascites develops over days)
  - Nausea/vomiting, fatigue, low-grade fever
  - Jaundice (late, suggests significant hepatic injury)
  - Encephalopathy (confusion, drowsiness) — red flag for acute liver failure
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Tender, smooth hepatomegaly (often massive, palpable below costal margin)
  - Ascites (shifting dullness, fluid thrill)
  - Splenomegaly (chronic portal hypertension)
  - Caput medusae or other abdominal wall collaterals
  - Lower limb oedema (if IVC involvement)
  - JVP – usually normal (differentiates from right heart failure)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Cirrhosis with decompensation:** no acute RUQ pain, chronic history, stigmata of chronic liver disease (spider naevi, palmar erythema)
  - **Alcoholic hepatitis:** jaundice + tender hepatomegaly + history of heavy alcohol use; AST>ALT ratio often >2
  - **Right heart failure:** raised JVP, peripheral oedema, no RUQ pain; responds to diuresis
  - **Portal vein thrombosis:** splenomegaly, varices, but no hepatomegaly; Doppler shows patent hepatic veins
  - **Acute cholecystitis:** Murphy’s sign, fever, no ascites; ultrasound shows gallbladder wall thickening
  - **Hepatic abscess:** fever, rigors, single/multiple lesions on imaging; aspiration diagnostic
- **Splitter:** JVP – raised in heart failure or constrictive pericarditis; normal in Budd-Chiari (unless IVC obstruction)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dip (exclude UTI as cause of fever, check for bilirubin)
  - Pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age – thrombophilia evaluation and treatment implications)
  - Capillary blood glucose (hypoglycaemia in acute liver failure)
  - Bedside ultrasound (if available) – look for hepatic vein flow with colour Doppler
- **Bloods:**
  - **LFTs:** AST/ALT often 2-5x ULN, ALP and GGT mildly raised; bilirubin variable
  - **Coagulation:** PT/INR prolonged (synthetic dysfunction)
  - **FBC:** thrombocytopenia (hypersplenism), anaemia, raised WCC (if SBP)
  - **U&E, creatinine, lactate:** assess renal function, metabolic acidosis
  - **Ammonia:** if encephalopathy
  - **Viral hepatitis serology, autoimmune screen, ferritin, caeruloplasmin:** exclude other causes of acute liver injury
  - **Thrombophilia screen:** protein C, protein S, antithrombin, factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation, antiphospholipid antibodies, JAK2 V617F (myeloproliferative neoplasm)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Doppler ultrasound of hepatic veins – absence of flow, reversed flow, or thrombus; also assess portal vein, IVC, and presence of collaterals (spider-web pattern)
  - **Second-line:** CT venography or MRI venography – define extent of thrombosis, identify caudate lobe hypertrophy (compensatory drainage) and malignancy
  - **Liver biopsy:** rarely needed (if imaging inconclusive, or to exclude cirrhosis); should be done after ascites drained and coagulopathy corrected
- **Special tests:**
  - Ascitic fluid analysis: SAAG >11 g/L (portal hypertension), neutrophil count >250 cells/mm³ = SBP
  - Endoscopy: screen for oesophageal varices before anticoagulation
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No formal scoring system; diagnosis confirmed by imaging showing hepatic vein outflow obstruction with clinical correlation.
- **Never-miss test:** **Doppler ultrasound of hepatic veins** – most patients with unexplained acute ascites + tender hepatomegaly should have this. Alternatively, a CT with portal venous phase if ultrasound unavailable.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - IV access, bloods (including group and save, coagulation), crossmatch if bleeding
  - Correct hypoglycaemia: 10% dextrose if BM <4
  - Treat suspected SBP: IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam or cefotaxime [check local policy]) after blood cultures and ascitic tap
  - If acute liver failure (encephalopathy + INR >2.0): discuss with transplant centre; transfer to HDU/ICU; give IV N-acetylcysteine (beneficial even if not paracetamol toxicity)
  - If variceal bleeding: Terlipressin 2mg IV bolus (then 1mg QDS) + prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone) + urgent upper GI endoscopy
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Anticoagulation:** start LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin) [check local policy] – dose adjusted for renal function. Aim for full therapeutic anticoagulation unless contraindicated (active bleeding, INR >2.5)
  - **Ascites management:** salt restriction (if not encephalopathic), spironolactone 100mg od + furosemide 40mg od (titrate), therapeutic paracentesis if tense (with albumin 8g/L ascites removed)
  - **Monitor for variceal bleeding:** start non-selective beta-blocker if varices present (propranolol 40mg bd, titrate to HR 55-60)
  - **Nutrition:** high-calorie, low-sodium diet; consider vitamin K 10mg IV once (if INR raised)
  - **Thrombophilia workup:** send before starting warfarin (anticoagulation will affect protein C/S levels)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Anticoagulation:** switch to warfarin (target INR 2-3) once stable; duration lifelong unless transient cause identified (e.g., pregnancy)
  - **Interventional radiology/surgery:** if refractory – transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) if portal hypertension dominant; surgical shunt or liver transplant if progressive liver failure
  - **Surveillance:** if chronic Budd-Chiari – screen for hepatocellular carcinoma (6-monthly ultrasound + AFP), variceal surveillance (endoscopy)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or Hartmann's at 1 L/24h (avoid aggressive fluids – worsen ascites/volume overload); 10% dextrose 1 L/24h if encephalopathic/hypoglycaemic
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low molecular weight heparin unless INR >2.0 (but these patients are already being therapeutically anticoagulated – do not over-anticoagulate)
  - **Diet:** low sodium (<2g/day) if ascites; NBM if encephalopathic or post-variceal bleed
  - **Observation frequency:** 4-hourly NEWS2, daily weight, strict fluid balance, daily LFTs/INR/ammonia until stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line anticoagulation:**
  - **Enoxaparin (LMWH):** 1.5 mg/kg once daily (treatment dose) or 1 mg/kg twice daily [check local policy] – adjust for creatinine clearance <30 mL/min
  - **Warfarin:** start 3-5mg od (check BNF) with daily INR; target INR 2-3
- **Ascites diuretics:**
  - Spironolactone 100 mg once daily (max 400 mg) + furosemide 40 mg once daily (max 160 mg)
- **SBP treatment (first-line):**
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV TDS [check local policy] or cefotaxime 2g IV TDS
- **Variceal bleeding:**
  - Terlipressin 2 mg IV bolus, then 1 mg QDS for up to 5 days
  - Ceftriaxone 1 g IV od for 7 days (prophylaxis)
- **N-acetylcysteine (acute liver failure):** 150 mg/kg in 200 mL 5% dextrose over 1 hour, then 50 mg/kg in 500 mL over 4 hours, then 100 mg/kg in 1 L over 16 hours (discuss with senior/transplant unit)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Anticoagulation: caution with thrombocytopenia (<50), active bleeding, recent surgery, large varices (screen first)
  - Diuretics: avoid in severe hyponatraemia (<125) or acute kidney injury
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - LMWH: dose reduction needed if CrCl <30 (use weight-based unfractionated heparin if severe)
  - Warfarin: no dose adjustment but INR more labile in liver disease – monitor frequently
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Warfarin loading by protocol in a patient with INR already elevated from cirrhosis** – check baseline INR before starting; giving full loading dose (10mg) can cause catastrophic bleeding. Start low (2-3mg) and titrate.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Acute liver failure (encephalopathy, coagulopathy, hypoglycaemia)
  - Variceal bleeding (can be torrential)
  - Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP)
  - Hepatic encephalopathy (often precipitated by infection, bleeding, constipation)
  - Acute kidney injury (hepatorenal syndrome)
- **Chronic:**
  - Cirrhosis and portal hypertension (ascites, varices, splenomegaly)
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma (increased risk even without cirrhosis)
  - Portal vein thrombosis (extension of thrombotic process)
  - Chronic liver failure and transplantation requirement
sections.Prognosis:
- **Acute:** high mortality (>50% if untreated) reduced to 30% with early anticoagulation and TIPS
- **Chronic:** 5-year survival 50-80% with appropriate management; death usually from liver failure or variceal bleeding. Liver transplant offers >70% 5-year survival.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform Doppler ultrasound of hepatic veins** in any patient with unexplained ascites and tender hepatomegaly – it will save a miss
- **Send thrombophilia screen before starting warfarin** (LMWH does not affect protein C/S levels, but warfarin does; you need baseline for accurate diagnosis)
- **Start treating SBP empirically** if ascites neutrophil count >250 cells/mm³; delay in antibiotics worsens mortality
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT assume ascites is simply decompensated cirrhosis** – if there is acute RUQ pain, always suspect outflow obstruction
- **Do NOT prescribe anticoagulation without first screening for oesophageal varices** – give non-selective beta-blocker or plan variceal banding before starting warfarin
- **Do NOT use lactulose to treat hepatic encephalopathy in acute Budd-Chiari** – it may worsen volume depletion and hyponatraemia; use rifaximin instead if needed (discuss with senior)
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening abdominal pain or distension (ascites, thrombosis extension)
  - Haematemesis or melaena (variceal bleed)
  - Fever, rigors, new confusion (SBP, encephalopathy)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Hepatology and haematology (anticoagulation clinic) within 2 weeks; imaging (US) at 3-6 months; variceal surveillance endoscopy at 1 year or earlier if symptoms
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - INR >2.0 and rising, or bleeding (war point to acute liver failure)
  - New hepatic encephalopathy (GCS <15, asterixis)
  - NEWS2 >5, lactate >4 mmol/L, rising creatinine
  - Hypotension not responding to fluid bolus (suspect variceal bleed or septic shock)
  - Need for TIPS or transplant discussion
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Intubated for airway protection (encephalopathy), vasopressors required, renal replacement therapy needed
  - Rapidly worsening lactate/INR despite initial management

rawMarkdown:
# Budd-Chiari Syndrome
**One-liner:** Hepatic vein outflow obstruction causing hepatic congestion, painful hepatomegaly, and ascites; rare but rapidly fatal if missed.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute liver failure:** asterixis + INR >2.0 + hypoglycaemia – this is not simple ascites; needs urgent senior review and transfer to HDU/ICU
- **Variceal bleeding:** haematemesis or melaena with haemodynamic instability – present in chronic Budd-Chiari with portal hypertension; require immediate resuscitation and endoscopic therapy
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP):** abdominal pain + fever + raised WCC in ascitic fluid – sepsis can tip into shock quickly; treat empirically before culture results

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Acute onset right upper quadrant (RUQ) pain (often severe, mimicking cholecystitis)
  - Rapidly increasing abdominal girth (ascites develops over days)
  - Nausea/vomiting, fatigue, low-grade fever
  - Jaundice (late, suggests significant hepatic injury)
  - Encephalopathy (confusion, drowsiness) — red flag for acute liver failure
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Tender, smooth hepatomegaly (often massive, palpable below costal margin)
  - Ascites (shifting dullness, fluid thrill)
  - Splenomegaly (chronic portal hypertension)
  - Caput medusae or other abdominal wall collaterals
  - Lower limb oedema (if IVC involvement)
  - JVP – usually normal (differentiates from right heart failure)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Cirrhosis with decompensation:** no acute RUQ pain, chronic history, stigmata of chronic liver disease (spider naevi, palmar erythema)
  - **Alcoholic hepatitis:** jaundice + tender hepatomegaly + history of heavy alcohol use; AST>ALT ratio often >2
  - **Right heart failure:** raised JVP, peripheral oedema, no RUQ pain; responds to diuresis
  - **Portal vein thrombosis:** splenomegaly, varices, but no hepatomegaly; Doppler shows patent hepatic veins
  - **Acute cholecystitis:** Murphy’s sign, fever, no ascites; ultrasound shows gallbladder wall thickening
  - **Hepatic abscess:** fever, rigors, single/multiple lesions on imaging; aspiration diagnostic
- **Splitter:** JVP – raised in heart failure or constrictive pericarditis; normal in Budd-Chiari (unless IVC obstruction)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dip (exclude UTI as cause of fever, check for bilirubin)
  - Pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age – thrombophilia evaluation and treatment implications)
  - Capillary blood glucose (hypoglycaemia in acute liver failure)
  - Bedside ultrasound (if available) – look for hepatic vein flow with colour Doppler
- **Bloods:**
  - **LFTs:** AST/ALT often 2-5x ULN, ALP and GGT mildly raised; bilirubin variable
  - **Coagulation:** PT/INR prolonged (synthetic dysfunction)
  - **FBC:** thrombocytopenia (hypersplenism), anaemia, raised WCC (if SBP)
  - **U&E, creatinine, lactate:** assess renal function, metabolic acidosis
  - **Ammonia:** if encephalopathy
  - **Viral hepatitis serology, autoimmune screen, ferritin, caeruloplasmin:** exclude other causes of acute liver injury
  - **Thrombophilia screen:** protein C, protein S, antithrombin, factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation, antiphospholipid antibodies, JAK2 V617F (myeloproliferative neoplasm)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Doppler ultrasound of hepatic veins – absence of flow, reversed flow, or thrombus; also assess portal vein, IVC, and presence of collaterals (spider-web pattern)
  - **Second-line:** CT venography or MRI venography – define extent of thrombosis, identify caudate lobe hypertrophy (compensatory drainage) and malignancy
  - **Liver biopsy:** rarely needed (if imaging inconclusive, or to exclude cirrhosis); should be done after ascites drained and coagulopathy corrected
- **Special tests:**
  - Ascitic fluid analysis: SAAG >11 g/L (portal hypertension), neutrophil count >250 cells/mm³ = SBP
  - Endoscopy: screen for oesophageal varices before anticoagulation
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No formal scoring system; diagnosis confirmed by imaging showing hepatic vein outflow obstruction with clinical correlation.
- **Never-miss test:** **Doppler ultrasound of hepatic veins** – most patients with unexplained acute ascites + tender hepatomegaly should have this. Alternatively, a CT with portal venous phase if ultrasound unavailable.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - IV access, bloods (including group and save, coagulation), crossmatch if bleeding
  - Correct hypoglycaemia: 10% dextrose if BM <4
  - Treat suspected SBP: IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam or cefotaxime [check local policy]) after blood cultures and ascitic tap
  - If acute liver failure (encephalopathy + INR >2.0): discuss with transplant centre; transfer to HDU/ICU; give IV N-acetylcysteine (beneficial even if not paracetamol toxicity)
  - If variceal bleeding: Terlipressin 2mg IV bolus (then 1mg QDS) + prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone) + urgent upper GI endoscopy
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Anticoagulation:** start LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin) [check local policy] – dose adjusted for renal function. Aim for full therapeutic anticoagulation unless contraindicated (active bleeding, INR >2.5)
  - **Ascites management:** salt restriction (if not encephalopathic), spironolactone 100mg od + furosemide 40mg od (titrate), therapeutic paracentesis if tense (with albumin 8g/L ascites removed)
  - **Monitor for variceal bleeding:** start non-selective beta-blocker if varices present (propranolol 40mg bd, titrate to HR 55-60)
  - **Nutrition:** high-calorie, low-sodium diet; consider vitamin K 10mg IV once (if INR raised)
  - **Thrombophilia workup:** send before starting warfarin (anticoagulation will affect protein C/S levels)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Anticoagulation:** switch to warfarin (target INR 2-3) once stable; duration lifelong unless transient cause identified (e.g., pregnancy)
  - **Interventional radiology/surgery:** if refractory – transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) if portal hypertension dominant; surgical shunt or liver transplant if progressive liver failure
  - **Surveillance:** if chronic Budd-Chiari – screen for hepatocellular carcinoma (6-monthly ultrasound + AFP), variceal surveillance (endoscopy)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or Hartmann's at 1 L/24h (avoid aggressive fluids – worsen ascites/volume overload); 10% dextrose 1 L/24h if encephalopathic/hypoglycaemic
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low molecular weight heparin unless INR >2.0 (but these patients are already being therapeutically anticoagulated – do not over-anticoagulate)
  - **Diet:** low sodium (<2g/day) if ascites; NBM if encephalopathic or post-variceal bleed
  - **Observation frequency:** 4-hourly NEWS2, daily weight, strict fluid balance, daily LFTs/INR/ammonia until stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line anticoagulation:**
  - **Enoxaparin (LMWH):** 1.5 mg/kg once daily (treatment dose) or 1 mg/kg twice daily [check local policy] – adjust for creatinine clearance <30 mL/min
  - **Warfarin:** start 3-5mg od (check BNF) with daily INR; target INR 2-3
- **Ascites diuretics:**
  - Spironolactone 100 mg once daily (max 400 mg) + furosemide 40 mg once daily (max 160 mg)
- **SBP treatment (first-line):**
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV TDS [check local policy] or cefotaxime 2g IV TDS
- **Variceal bleeding:**
  - Terlipressin 2 mg IV bolus, then 1 mg QDS for up to 5 days
  - Ceftriaxone 1 g IV od for 7 days (prophylaxis)
- **N-acetylcysteine (acute liver failure):** 150 mg/kg in 200 mL 5% dextrose over 1 hour, then 50 mg/kg in 500 mL over 4 hours, then 100 mg/kg in 1 L over 16 hours (discuss with senior/transplant unit)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Anticoagulation: caution with thrombocytopenia (<50), active bleeding, recent surgery, large varices (screen first)
  - Diuretics: avoid in severe hyponatraemia (<125) or acute kidney injury
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - LMWH: dose reduction needed if CrCl <30 (use weight-based unfractionated heparin if severe)
  - Warfarin: no dose adjustment but INR more labile in liver disease – monitor frequently
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Warfarin loading by protocol in a patient with INR already elevated from cirrhosis** – check baseline INR before starting; giving full loading dose (10mg) can cause catastrophic bleeding. Start low (2-3mg) and titrate.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Acute liver failure (encephalopathy, coagulopathy, hypoglycaemia)
  - Variceal bleeding (can be torrential)
  - Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP)
  - Hepatic encephalopathy (often precipitated by infection, bleeding, constipation)
  - Acute kidney injury (hepatorenal syndrome)
- **Chronic:**
  - Cirrhosis and portal hypertension (ascites, varices, splenomegaly)
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma (increased risk even without cirrhosis)
  - Portal vein thrombosis (extension of thrombotic process)
  - Chronic liver failure and transplantation requirement

### Prognosis
- **Acute:** high mortality (>50% if untreated) reduced to 30% with early anticoagulation and TIPS
- **Chronic:** 5-year survival 50-80% with appropriate management; death usually from liver failure or variceal bleeding. Liver transplant offers >70% 5-year survival.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform Doppler ultrasound of hepatic veins** in any patient with unexplained ascites and tender hepatomegaly – it will save a miss
- **Send thrombophilia screen before starting warfarin** (LMWH does not affect protein C/S levels, but warfarin does; you need baseline for accurate diagnosis)
- **Start treating SBP empirically** if ascites neutrophil count >250 cells/mm³; delay in antibiotics worsens mortality

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT assume ascites is simply decompensated cirrhosis** – if there is acute RUQ pain, always suspect outflow obstruction
- **Do NOT prescribe anticoagulation without first screening for oesophageal varices** – give non-selective beta-blocker or plan variceal banding before starting warfarin
- **Do NOT use lactulose to treat hepatic encephalopathy in acute Budd-Chiari** – it may worsen volume depletion and hyponatraemia; use rifaximin instead if needed (discuss with senior)

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening abdominal pain or distension (ascites, thrombosis extension)
  - Haematemesis or melaena (variceal bleed)
  - Fever, rigors, new confusion (SBP, encephalopathy)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Hepatology and haematology (anticoagulation clinic) within 2 weeks; imaging (US) at 3-6 months; variceal surveillance endoscopy at 1 year or earlier if symptoms

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - INR >2.0 and rising, or bleeding (war point to acute liver failure)
  - New hepatic encephalopathy (GCS <15, asterixis)
  - NEWS2 >5, lactate >4 mmol/L, rising creatinine
  - Hypotension not responding to fluid bolus (suspect variceal bleed or septic shock)
  - Need for TIPS or transplant discussion
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Intubated for airway protection (encephalopathy), vasopressors required, renal replacement therapy needed
  - Rapidly worsening lactate/INR despite initial management
